truncated_options.passage_text: 2. At Oakwood Elementary School, there is a special teaching assistant named Robo-Teach. This small, blue-and-white robot helps students with their lessons and makes learning fun. Robo-Teach can move around the classroom, answer questions, and even tell jokes to keep kids smiling. Robo-Teach has become a favourite at Oakwood Elementary. Students look forward to seeing it every day, and even shy kids feel comfortable asking it questions. The robot may not replace human teachers, but it sure makes school a lot more fun and helps everyone learn better. Who wouldn’t want a robot friend like Robo-Teach?

truncated_options.passage_text: 3. Quan họ is a beautiful form of traditional folk singing from northern Vietnam, known for its gentle melodies and poetic lyrics. For many years, this art form was fading as younger generations became less interested. To keep quan họ alive, a special programme was created to teach and promote it. The goal is to make sure this important part of Vietnam’s culture does not disappear. The programme includes workshops where master singers teach young people the songs and techniques of quan họ. Schools and community centres also hold performances so everyone can enjoy this music. By making learning fun and interactive, the programme encourages children and teenagers to take part. Many young singers now proudly perform quan họ at festivals and competitions. Local leaders and artists are working together to support this revival. They organise events where quan họ singers perform in villages, reminding people of its beauty. Some groups even mix quan họ with modern music to attract younger audiences. Thanks to these efforts, more people are starting to appreciate this traditional art form again. The programme has brought new energy to quan họ, helping it stay relevant in today’s world. Families now sing these old songs together, and villages celebrate their cultural heritage with pride. By passing quan họ to the next generation, Vietnam ensures that this unique music will continue to be loved for years to come.

At Greenfield High School, students and teachers have joined forces to fight smoking through their 
"Breathe Free" campaign. The programme started after a school survey showed that many students didn't 
understand how quickly smoking could damage their health. Now, colourful posters with facts about 
smoking dangers fill the hallways, and the student council organises monthly "Health Days" with fun 
activities about clean living.   
The campaign's most powerful event is the annual "Real Stories" assembly, where former smokers 
share how cigarettes hurt their lives. Last year, a local basketball player moved students to tears when he 
described losing his athletic scholarship due to smoking-related health problems. The school nurse also 
runs a popular "Quit Club" that meets every Thursday, offering support and healthy alternatives like 
chewing gum or stress balls.   
Greenfield High School has taken strong steps to enforce no-smoking rules. The school installed 
new smoke detectors near bathrooms and the back field where students used to sneak cigarettes. Instead 
of just punishing smokers, the campaign focuses on helping them. Students caught smoking must attend 
special counselling sessions and participate in creating anti-smoking materials for younger kids.   
After two years, "Breathe Free" is making a real difference. School health reports show 40% fewer 
students admitting to smoking, and the student-run "Fresh Air Squad" keeps spreading the message. Even 
local shops near the school have agreed not to sell cigarettes to Greenfield students. What began as a small 
health project has grown into a community-wide movement for cleaner lungs and brighter futures.

truncated_options.passage_text: From melting arctic ice caps to forest fires, global warming is heating up the planet and damaging the Earth's ecosystems. Global warming is the rise of the Earth's temperatures caused by an increase of CO2 and other greenhouse gas emissions. Global warming has increased very much in the past 100 years because people are using more and more electricity and driving more cars. We are also cutting down more trees that clean the air. Scientists have been studying the effects of global warming on different ecosystems for a long time and what they have found is quite worrying. The rising temperatures have caused many problems in ecosystems. Arctic ice is melting, causing sea levels to rise and floods in seaside areas. As a result, animals and plants are losing their homes. As oceans and rivers become warmer, the water temperatures keep changing. Some marine animals cannot live in these new conditions. Since the 1970s, populations of fish in lakes and rivers have decreased by 76 percent. Global warming is also causing extreme weather events. Other ecosystems, like deserts and grasslands, are experiencing terrible droughts and certain wildlife and plant species that depend on water are dying. Due to the high temperatures, wildfires are destroying whole ecosystems such as forests. There has been a serious rise in endangered species in recent years that could result in many animals becoming extinct. The loss of plants and animals causes problems for all living things in an ecosystem. We need to take action to stop global warming and save the Earth's ecosystems before it's too late. (Adapted from Bright)

truncated_options.passage_text: Many people have learning disorders. They suffer in school: other children bully them or make fun of them and call them 'slow'. However, people can get over their learning disorders and have successful careers with the help and support of family, teachers, and doctors, as these examples prove. At school, Michael Phelps could not sit still and found it difficult to concentrate. But then he found his passion for sports. His reading improved when he read books about sports and when the problems reflected his interests. Through hard work and never missing a day's practice, Michael became an Olympic champion, winning twenty-eight medals, twenty-three of them gold. In the world of autism, Dr Temple Grandin is an important voice. Autistic herself, she is an expert on how to teach people with autism, and she has written six books about the condition. School was difficult for her because she didn't think in the same way as other children. At school, her science teacher became her mentor. With his help and support, Grandin became interested in animal science. She later became a professor of animal science at Colorado State University. Keira Knightley found out she had dyslexia at the age of six. She believes that her problems with reading and writing made her strong. Knightley struggled with reading and writing, but she dreamt of being an actress, so she had to read scripts. She had to work harder than other children to improve her literacy. Later she finished school with top grades. So don't worry if you are different - you never know where your differences could lead you. (Adapted from English Discovery)

truncated_options.passage_text: Rachel Carson, an ecologist, a biologist, and a writer, was born in 1907 in Springdale, Pennsylvania. As a small girl, she was an avid reader and soon showed a keen interest in the natural world and writing. She had her first story about animals published in a magazine when she was in the fourth grade. She graduated from Pennsylvania College for Women (now Chatham University) in 1929, studied at the Woods Hole Marine Biological Laboratory, and received her MA in zoology from Johns Hopkins University in 1932. Initially, Rachel Carson worked for the US Bureau of Fisheries as a part-time science writer. Part of her job was to interest the public in marine and freshwater biology via radio programmes. She then spent the next few years serving as a marine scientist and editor for the US Fish and Wildlife Service. There, she became famous for her writings on environmental pollution and the natural history of the sea, in which she always concerned herself about the impact that humans made on the natural world. Climate change, rising sea-levels, melting Arctic glaciers, decreasing animal populations are part of her work. In her most influential book, Silent Spring (1962), Rachel Carson strongly disapproved of the widespread use of pesticides such as DDT. She warned the public about its negative long-term effects and called for new policies to protect humans and the environment. She then was criticised by the chemical industry, but never gave up. The book eventually prompted a change in national policy, leading to the enactment of a national ban of pesticides on agricultural farming. Additionally, it helped spark the environmental movement, resulting in the establishment of the United States Environmental Protection Agency. That's why she was called the mother of modern ecology. Rachel Carson died of breast cancer in 1964. However, her work continues to inspire new generations to protect all the living world. (Adapted from Friends Global)

truncated_options.passage_text: Ben Silbermann was an ordinary boy. He was born in 1982 in Iowa in the USA and he loved collecting things; stamps, leaves, and especially insects. As a young man in his 20s, his love of collecting turned into big business. [I] He went to Yale University and studied chemistry and political science. [II] In 2006, when he graduated from university, he moved to the state of California and got a job at Google. [III] Silbermann met a lot of people there who were very interested in technology, and he decided on two things. [IV] He worked at Google for two years. Then, in 2008, Silbermann left his job and he started his own business with two friends: Paul Sciarra and Evan Sharp. In November 2009, they got an apartment together in Palo Alto, California and started work on Pinterest. Silbermann and Sharp realised they both loved collecting, and they made a "virtual pinboard" - pages online where you "pin" pictures of things you like and things you "collect". Their idea was to connect everyone in the world through the things they find interesting. And it seemed that a lot of people liked their idea. When Pinterest started in March 2010, it grew quickly and had 10 million users after the first year. This was an amazing number compared with Twitter and Facebook: Twitter took two years to reach 10 million users and Facebook took over five years. Just a year after starting, Pinterest was worth $200 million and had over 421 million page views. Silbermann's ordinary interest as a boy certainly became an extraordinary business. (Adapted from English 12)

truncated_options.passage_text: Ben Silbermann was an ordinary boy. He was born in 1982 in Iowa in the USA and he loved collecting things; stamps, leaves, and especially insects. As a young man in his 20s, his love of collecting turned into big business. [I] He went to Yale University and studied chemistry and political science. [II] In 2006, when he graduated from university, he moved to the state of California and got a job at Google. [III] Silbermann met a lot of people there who were very interested in technology, and he decided on two things. [IV] He worked at Google for two years. Then, in 2008, Silbermann left his job and he started his own business with two friends: Paul Sciarra and Evan Sharp. In November 2009, they got an apartment together in Palo Alto, California and started work on Pinterest. Silbermann and Sharp realised they both loved collecting, and they made a "virtual pinboard" - pages online where you "pin" pictures of things you like and things you "collect". Their idea was to connect everyone in the world through the things they find interesting. And it seemed that a lot of people liked their idea. When Pinterest started in March 2010, it grew quickly and had 10 million users after the first year. This was an amazing number compared with Twitter and Facebook: Twitter took two years to reach 10 million users and Facebook took over five years. Just a year after starting, Pinterest was worth $200 million and had over 421 million page views. Silbermann's ordinary interest as a boy certainly became an extraordinary business. (Adapted from English 12)

possible_wrong_passage_pairing.passage_text: Read the following passages and mark the letter A, B, C, or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best answer to each of the following questions. No animal species can survive indefinitely on the Earth. Centuries ago, species went extinct from natural causes, for example, they were unable to adapt to bad weather and other difficult conditions. However, animals are now dying out faster than ever because of human activity. It is estimated that, until the 18th century, one species disappeared from the Earth every four years. By the 19th century, this had increased to one species per year. By 1975, it was 1,000 species per year, and today, animals are disappearing at the alarming rate of more than 40,000 species per year. Many factors can cause an animal or plant species to become endangered, and one big one is the destruction of their habitats. Deforestation and soil, air, and water pollution are usually the main culprits. For example, the population of critically Sumatran orangutans is now less than 10,000 on their home island of Sumatra, Indonesia, due to deforestation and farming. Another cause of endangerment is humans exploiting wild animals. Uncontrolled hunting of whales in the last century, for example, resulted in many whale species becoming critically endangered. The high demands for animal parts stem from their use in foods or medicines or their value as decorative objects. For example, the ivory tusks of elephants are used to make jewellery, and the price is high enough that people risk being arrested and jailed to go after these animals. (Adapted from English Workbook 12)

reading_true_false_not_structured.question_text: Many zero-posters likely believe sharing online inevitably exposes them to unwanted judgement or scrutiny.
reading_true_false_not_structured.passage_text: Read the following passage and do the task below. In the dynamic landscape of social media, a curious trend is quietly gaining traction among young people: the practice of posting zero content. Known as “digital lurking” or “zero-posting,” this involves maintaining an active account to consume content, follow trends, and stay connected, while deliberately never sharing personal updates, photos, or opinions. This stands in stark contrast to the previous generation’s impulse to curate and broadcast their lives online meticulously. Several compelling motivations underpin this shift towards online invisibility. A primary driver is a growing consciousness of privacy and data security. Many youths, wary of permanent digital footprints and sophisticated algorithms, choose to minimise their online exposure. Furthermore, the pressure to perform a perfect, aesthetically pleasing life can be exhausting. Abstaining from posting becomes a form of resistance against the anxiety of constant comparison and the performative nature of modern platforms. It is a deliberate rejection of the validation-seeking cycle of likes and comments. The implications of this silent engagement are multifaceted. For individuals, it can foster a healthier relationship with social media, reducing stress and freeing up mental space. However, it also creates a passive audience that consumes without contributing, which can paradoxically reinforce the very influence of the prolific content creators and commercial entities they observe. From a societal perspective, if large segments of a generation disengage from public discourse online, it could subtly reshape the nature of digital communities, making them less about peer interaction and more about one-way broadcasting. Ultimately, the zero-poster trend signifies a critical evolution in digital literacy. It is not a rejection of technology, but a more strategic and intentional way of navigating it. Young people are moving from being enthusiastic content creators to becoming cautious curators of their own digital well-being. This conscious silence speaks volumes about a generation prioritising mental peace and personal privacy over public acclaim, redefining what it means to truly “participate” in the virtual world. Which of the following can be inferred from the passage? Decide each of the following statements is TRUE (T) or FALSE (F).

Read the following passage and do the task below. A distinct and colourful trend is reshaping local calendars worldwide: the enthusiastic adoption of foreign festivals. From Diwali illuminations in European capitals to Halloween parades in Asian metropolises, people are increasingly looking beyond their traditional heritage to participate in global celebrations. This phenomenon transcends mere novelty; it reflects a deeper shift in our interconnected world, where cultural boundaries are becoming more porous. Individuals are actively curating their own hybrid traditions, blending the familiar with the fascinating unknown from afar. Several profound factors propel this cultural infusion. Primarily, globalisation and digital connectivity have dismantled geographical barriers. Through films, social media, and diaspora communities, knowledge of vibrant celebrations like Holi or Lunar New Year becomes accessible and appealing. Furthermore, in multicultural urban societies, these festivals offer a tangible way to express inclusivity and connect with neighbours from diverse backgrounds. They provide a shared, joyous experience that fosters social cohesion and mutual understanding, moving beyond theoretical tolerance to active, celebratory participation. However, this trend is not without its nuanced complexities. Critics raise valid concerns about cultural appropriation—the superficial or disrespectful borrowing of sacred symbols without understanding their profound significance. When a festival is stripped of its religious or historical context and reduced to an aesthetic theme party, it risks causing offence and eroding its authentic meaning. The challenge, therefore, lies in navigating the fine line between appreciative adoption and exploitative appropriation. True celebration requires a degree of mindful engagement and respect for the festival’s origins. Ultimately, the growing embrace of foreign festivals signifies a collective yearning for richer communal experiences in an increasingly digital age. When approached with sensitivity and a genuine desire to learn, it can be a powerful force for good. It cultivates cosmopolitanism, broadens perspectives, and creates a global tapestry of shared joy. This trend suggests that in the future, cultural literacy may be measured not only by knowledge of one’s own traditions but also by a respectful appreciation for the celebratory heritage of all humankind.

Known as “digital lurking” or “zero-posting,” this involves maintaining an active account to consume content, follow trends, and stay connected, while deliberately never sharing personal updates, photos, or opinions. This stands in stark contrast to the previous generation’s impulse to curate and broadcast their lives online meticulously. Several compelling motivations underpin this shift towards online invisibility. A primary driver is a growing consciousness of privacy and data security. Many youths, wary of permanent digital footprints and sophisticated algorithms, choose to minimise their online exposure. Furthermore, the pressure to perform a perfect, aesthetically pleasing life can be exhausting. Abstaining from posting becomes a form of resistance against the anxiety of constant comparison and the performative nature of modern platforms. It is a deliberate rejection of the validation-seeking cycle of likes and comments. The implications of this silent engagement are multifaceted. For individuals, it can foster a healthier relationship with social media, reducing stress and freeing up mental space. However, it also creates a passive audience that consumes without contributing, which can paradoxically reinforce the very influence of the prolific content creators and commercial entities they observe. From a societal perspective, if large segments of a generation disengage from public discourse online, it could subtly reshape the nature of digital communities, making them less about peer interaction and more about one-way broadcasting. Ultimately, the zero-poster trend signifies a critical evolution in digital literacy. It is not a rejection of technology, but a more strategic and intentional way of navigating it. Young people are moving from being enthusiastic content creators to becoming cautious curators of their own digital well-being. This conscious silence speaks volumes about a generation prioritising mental peace and personal privacy over public acclaim, redefining what it means to truly “participate” in the virtual world. Which of the following can be inferred from the passage? Decide each of the following statements is TRUE (T) or FALSE (F).
